summary: German-born Empress of Russia (r. 1762-1796) and the country's longest-serving female ruler, who seized the throne in a 1762 coup against her husband Peter III with the help of her lover Grigory Orlov and his brothers. Styled an "enlightened despot," she corresponded with Voltaire, issued the Nakaz to guide a Legislative Commission, and founded the Smolny Institute for women's education, even as she crushed Pugachev's Rebellion (a serf uprising led by a Peter III impersonator) and expanded serfdom. She fought two wars against the Ottoman Empire that won Russia the northern Black Sea coast and Crimea (with the help of her chief advisor and lover Grigory Potemkin, of "Potemkin villages" fame), and orchestrated, with Prussia and Austria, the partitions of Poland-Lithuania that erased the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth from the map.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How Catherine is identified in giveaways: an "enlightened despot" and the longest-serving female Empress of Russia, who came to power via a 1762 coup against her husband Peter III and took many lovers, including Grigory Orlov, Grigory Potemkin, and the future Polish king Stanislaw Poniatowski.

clues.clue: Styled an "enlightened despot" and the longest-serving Empress of Russia
examples: name this enlightened despot and longest-serving empress of Russia.
Kosciuszko's Uprising occurred during the reign of this enlightened despot and Empress of Russia who was advised by Grigory Potemkin.

clues.clue: Had many lovers/favorites, including Grigory Orlov, Grigory Potemkin, and the future Polish king Stanislaw Poniatowski
examples: name this "Great" Russian Empress with many lovers.
Name this ruler who had many lovers including the last king of Poland, Stanislaw Poniatowski and Grigoriy Potemkin, who oversaw Russia's expansion into the Crimea during her reign.

works.name: The 1762 Coup Against Peter III
works.description: Catherine seized the throne in a 1762 coup against her husband, Peter III (nephew and successor of Empress Elizabeth), whose death ended Russia's involvement in the Seven Years' War. The coup was carried out with the help of her lover Grigory Orlov, an artillery commander, and his brothers, including Alexei Orlov; Peter III was killed shortly afterward.

clues.clue: Overthrew her husband Peter III in a 1762 coup, carried out with the help of the Orlov brothers, including her lover Grigory Orlov (an artillery commander and first leader of the Free Economic Society)
examples: this ruler came to power in a coup against Peter III, her spouse.
This artillery commander and lover of Catherine organized the coup that overthrew Paul III [sic, Peter III].

clues.clue: Peter III's death (shortly after being overthrown) ended Russia's involvement in the Seven Years' War
examples: Name this ruler, whose death ended Russia's involvement in the Seven Years' War.

clues.clue: Peter III was Empress Elizabeth's nephew and successor; Catherine succeeded him after his assassination
examples: Elizabeth died childless, and was succeeded by her nephew, Peter III. Peter was overthrown shortly thereafter by this wife of his, who reigned as a 'Great' czarina with the assistance of her minister.

works.name: Domestic Reforms: the Nakaz, Smolny Institute, and the Free Economic Society
works.description: Catherine styled herself an Enlightenment ruler through several domestic initiatives. She compiled the Nakaz, a legal document (criticized by Denis Diderot in his Observations) that guided a Legislative Commission and abolished the death penalty, and created the Assignation Bank, which issued Russia's first paper currency ("assignation" notes) backed by copper. She confirmed the Manifesto on Freedom of the Nobility, and, at the urging of Ivan Betskoy, opened the Smolny Institute, Russia's first institution for women's education. She also sponsored the Free Economic Society's inaugural essay competition, which asked what property rights peasants should have.

clues.clue: Created the Assignation Bank, which issued Russia's first paper currency ("assignation" notes) backed by copper
examples: This monarch ordered the creation of the Assignation Bank, which issued paper bills backed by copper currency.
This ruler created the Assignation Bank, the first to print currency in its nation.

clues.clue: Compiled the Nakaz, a new law code that abolished the death penalty and guided the Legislative Commission; Diderot criticized it in his Observations
examples: This ruler also compiled the Nakaz, a new law code which abolished the death penalty.
Diderot criticized Catherine in his Observations on this Enlightenment-inspired text which she wrote to guide the work of the Legislative Commission.

clues.clue: At the urging of Ivan Betskoy, opened the Smolny Institute, the first Russian institution for women's education
examples: At the urging of Ivan Betskoy, this ruler opened the Smolny Institute for women's education.
This empress of Russia included Grigory Orlov and Grigory Potemkin as many of her favorites. Under her reign, the Smolny institute, the first institute for women, was founded.

clues.clue: Confirmed the Manifesto on Freedom of the Nobility
examples: This ruler created the Assignation Bank, which issued the country's first paper money, and confirmed the Manifesto on Freedom of the Nobility.

clues.clue: At her suggestion, the Free Economic Society's inaugural essay competition asked what property rights peasants should have
examples: At this monarch's suggestion, a prompt asking what property rights peasants should have was used for the inaugural essay competition run by the Free Economic Society.

works.name: Pugachev's Rebellion (1773-1775)
works.description: Catherine's reign was shaken by Pugachev's Rebellion, a Cossack-led uprising against serfdom by Yemelyan Pugachev, who claimed to be her murdered husband Peter III and proclaimed freedom for serfs. Pugachev's forces sacked Kazan before he was defeated by Ivan Michelson and forced across the Volga in 1774; he was ultimately captured, beheaded, and quartered. A local legend holds that a lieutenant of Pugachev's watched a city burn from the Mari forest beneath his namesake oak tree after his own defeat.

clues.clue: Yemelyan Pugachev, a Cossack, led the 1773-75 rebellion against serfdom while impersonating her murdered husband Peter III
examples: Name this leader of a 1773 to 1775 rebellion who proclaimed a government opposed to serfdom while pretending to be Peter III.
The Cossack Yemelyan Pugachev challenged Catherine's reign and proclaimed the freedom of this class of people.

clues.clue: Pugachev's forces sacked Kazan before he was defeated by Ivan Michelson and forced to flee across the Volga in 1774, ending the rebellion
examples: Kazan was sacked during Pugachev's Rebellion, which took place during this empress' reign.
Pugachev was forced to escape across the Volga after being defeated by Ivan Michelson in battles around this city in 1774, which then led to the end of the rebellion.

clues.clue: After a defeat, a leader of the uprising supposedly watched a city burn from the Mari forest under his namesake oak tree, and was later beheaded and quartered
examples: After a defeat, the leader of an uprising against this ruler supposedly watched a city burn from the Mari forest under his namesake oak tree; that leader was beheaded and cut into four pieces.

works.name: Wars with the Ottoman Empire and the Annexation of Crimea
works.description: Catherine fought two wars against the Ottoman Empire that pushed Russia's border to the Black Sea. The first (1768-74) ended with the Treaty of Kuchuk Kainarji, confirmed at Jassy, which gave Russia port cities like Kerch and made the Crimean Khanate nominally independent (soon annexed outright in 1783); its naval highlight was the Battle of Cesme, won by Alexei Orlov, part of an Orlov-led revolt in Greece that anticipated Catherine's later "Greek Plan" to partition the Ottoman Empire and install her grandson Constantine over a revived Byzantine state. During her famous 1787 tour of the newly annexed Crimea and New Russia, her advisor and lover Grigory Potemkin allegedly erected fake settlements known as "Potemkin villages," and wives and daughters of the Greek Infantry Battalion were organized into an "Amazon Company" to greet her. American naval officer John Paul Jones served in her navy during the second Russo-Turkish War (1787).

clues.clue: The 1768-74 war with the Ottoman Empire ended with the Treaty of Kuchuk Kainarji (confirmed at Jassy), which gained Russia port cities like Kerch and made the Crimean Khanate nominally independent (annexed outright in 1783)
examples: This monarch gained port cities such as Kerch through an unequal peace that was confirmed by the Treaty of Jassy and signed in the Bulgarian village of Kuchuk Kainarji.
In 1783, Russia annexed this region under the direction of Catherine's diplomat Grigory Potemkin.

clues.clue: During her 1787 tour of Crimea, her advisor Grigory Potemkin allegedly erected fake settlements known as "Potemkin villages"; wives and daughters of the Greek Infantry Battalion formed an "Amazon Company" to greet her
examples: identify this ruler for whom fake settlements known as 'Potemkin Villages' were erected during her 1787 visit to the Crimea that she annexed.
One hundred wives and daughters of the Greek Infantry Battalion were enlisted to receive this ruler as the 'Amazon Company' during a tour of the Tauric provinces.

clues.clue: Formulated the "Greek Plan" to partition the Ottoman Empire and install her grandson (Grand Duke Constantine) over a revived neo-Byzantine state; her lover Grigory Orlov's brother Alexei won the naval Battle of Cesme as part of a related revolt in Greece
examples: This ruler formulated the 'Greek Plan' to partition the Ottoman Empire and was the lover of Grigory Orlov.
The Greek Plan would have entailed this grandson of Catherine ruling a neo-Byzantine state.

clues.clue: American naval officer John Paul Jones served in her navy during the 1787 Russo-Turkish War, feuding with the Prince of Nassau-Siegen
examples: This American officer served in Catherine's navy during the Russo-Turkish War of 1787, in which he feuded with the prince of Nassau-Siegen.
Another person employed by Catherine the Great was this naval commander, who commanded the Vladmir during the Russo-Turkish War.

clues.clue: Forcibly disbanded the Zaporozhian Host/Sich after the 1774 war ended in her favor, and her wars in Crimea were partly motivated to end raids by the Crimean Khanate and Nogai Horde
examples: This empress ordered the destruction of the Zaporizhian Sich after the Russo-Turkish War ended in her favor in 1774.
Catherine's wars in Crimea were in part motivated to end these events that the Crimean Khanate and the Nogai Horde had undertaken in the Wild Fields.

works.name: The Partitions of Poland
works.description: Catherine installed her former lover Stanislaw August Poniatowski as King of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth in 1764. She backed the conservative, pro-Russian Targowica Confederation against the reformist Constitution of May 3, 1791 (passed by the Great/Four-Year Sejm), and had her general Alexander Suvorov crush the Kosciuszko Uprising of 1794. The resulting Third Partition of Poland in 1795 erased the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth from the map and forced Poniatowski's abdication.

clues.clue: Installed her former lover Stanislaw August Poniatowski as King of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth in 1764; his abdication after the partitions of Poland ended the Commonwealth
examples: Identify this man who was installed as the King of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth in 1764 following the death of Augustus III.
Catherine became the lover of this future Polish king at age 26. He could not prevent the partitions of Poland, which ended with this man's abdication.

clues.clue: Backed the conservative, pro-Russian Targowica Confederation, which opposed the reformist Constitution of May 3, 1791 passed by the Great Sejm
examples: The Confederation of Bar opposed this monarch's ally Stanislaus II.
This Czarina intervened against the Constitution of May 3rd, 1791 at the behest of the Targowica Confederation.

clues.clue: Had her general Alexander Suvorov (who had stormed the Ottoman fort at Izmail) crush the Kosciuszko Uprising of 1794
examples: Kosciuszko's Uprising was put down by this general, who escaped Andre Massena in Italy by leading an army over the Swiss Alps.
Name this leader who led the Polish Uprising of 1794.

clues.clue: The 1795 Third Partition of Poland formally ended the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth
examples: a victory against the Kosciuszko Uprising led to the third Partition of Poland.
The Third Partition of Poland formally ended this political entity.

works.name: Enlightenment Correspondence and Critics
works.description: Catherine cultivated an image as an Enlightenment monarch, corresponding with Voltaire and inviting Denis Diderot to critique her Nakaz. She personally read and criticized Alexander Radishchev's Journey from St. Petersburg to Moscow, a polemical work attacking serfdom, and had it banned; despite her Enlightenment leanings, she never moved to emancipate the serfs. The self-styled psychic and Freemason Cagliostro (Giuseppe Balsamo) failed to win her patronage during his travels through European courts.

clues.clue: Corresponded with Voltaire
examples: This tsarina who corresponded with Voltaire counted Grigory Potemkin among her lovers.

clues.clue: Personally read and criticized Alexander Radishchev's Journey from St. Petersburg to Moscow, a polemic against serfdom, and had it banned, despite never moving to emancipate the serfs herself
examples: Radishchev's Journey was published during the reign of this empress, who read and criticized it. Although she had been heavily influenced by Enlightenment philosophy, she did not call for the emancipation [of serfs].
Identify this polemical work by Aleksandr Radishchev which attacks the institution of serfdom in Russia along with various other government policies. This book was quickly banned.

clues.clue: The self-styled psychic and Freemason Cagliostro (Giuseppe Balsamo) failed to gain her patronage while touring European courts
examples: During his years bopping around European courts acting as a psychic healer and magician, he failed to gain patronage with this 'great' Russian Tsarina who gained the throne after the assassination of her husband.

works.name: Legacy: Paul I's Succession
works.description: Catherine was succeeded by her son, the eccentric Paul I, whose reign quickly unraveled: he organized the League of Armed Neutrality, which the British saw as a threat and which Admiral Nelson crushed at the Battle of Copenhagen, and he was assassinated in 1801 in a plot usually named for Count Pahlen.

clues.clue: Her son and successor Paul I organized the League of Armed Neutrality, seen as a threat by Britain and destroyed by Admiral Nelson at the Battle of Copenhagen
examples: Catherine's son and successor, the eccentric Paul I, organized this alliance, which the British saw as a threat, resulting in Admiral Nelson destroying its fleet at the Battle of Copenhagen.

clues.clue: Paul I was assassinated in 1801 in a plot usually named for Count Pahlen, who stormed St. Michael's Palace and found the emperor hiding behind drapes
examples: Tsar Paul I was assassinated by this man, for whom the plot is usually named. He and General Yashvil stormed St. Michael's Palace, finding the emperor cowardly hiding behind some drapes.

comprehensive_summary: Catherine the Great, a German-born princess, became Empress of Russia in 1762 by overthrowing her husband Peter III (Empress Elizabeth's nephew and successor, whose death ended Russia's role in the Seven Years' War) in a coup carried out with her lover Grigory Orlov, an artillery commander, and his brothers. She styled herself an Enlightenment ruler: she corresponded with Voltaire, compiled the Nakaz (a legal document criticized by Denis Diderot's Observations) to guide a Legislative Commission and abolish the death penalty, opened the Smolny Institute for women's education at Ivan Betskoy's urging, created the Assignation Bank to issue Russia's first paper currency, confirmed the Manifesto on Freedom of the Nobility, and sponsored the Free Economic Society's inaugural essay competition on peasant property rights. Yet serfdom expanded under her rule, provoking Pugachev's Rebellion (1773-75), in which the Cossack Yemelyan Pugachev, impersonating the murdered Peter III, led an uprising that sacked Kazan before being crushed by Ivan Michelson; Catherine also personally read, criticized, and banned Alexander Radishchev's Journey from St. Petersburg to Moscow for its attack on serfdom, without ever emancipating the serfs herself. Militarily, she fought two wars against the Ottoman Empire, winning the northern Black Sea coast and Kerch at the Treaty of Kuchuk Kainarji (1774) and formally annexing Crimea in 1783; her advisor and lover Grigory Potemkin allegedly staged fake "Potemkin villages" during her 1787 tour of the region, while her Greek Plan envisioned installing her grandson Constantine over a revived Byzantine state, building on an earlier Orlov-led revolt in Greece capped by Alexei Orlov's naval victory at Cesme. The American naval hero John Paul Jones briefly served in her navy during the second Russo-Turkish War. In Poland, she installed her former lover Stanislaw August Poniatowski as king in 1764, then backed the conservative Targowica Confederation against his reformist Constitution of May 3, 1791, and had General Alexander Suvorov crush the 1794 Kosciuszko Uprising, leading to the 1795 Third Partition that erased the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth. She was succeeded by her son Paul I, whose brief, erratic reign -- marked by the League of Armed Neutrality (destroyed by Nelson at Copenhagen) -- ended in his 1801 assassination.
recursive_suggestions: Grigory Potemkin -- Catherine's chief advisor and lover; central to the Crimea annexation and 'Potemkin villages'
Yemelyan Pugachev -- leader of Pugachev's Rebellion, a major standalone figure/event
Peter III of Russia -- Catherine's husband, overthrown in 1762; distinct History figure topic
Stanislaw August Poniatowski -- last king of Poland, Catherine's former lover; distinct figure topic
Partitions of Poland -- could support its own dedicated page distinct from Catherine's biography
Alexander Radishchev -- author of the banned Journey from St. Petersburg to Moscow
Paul I of Russia -- Catherine's son and successor; distinct figure topic
Tadeusz Kosciuszko -- leader of the 1794 uprising against Russian control of Poland
